bgp update-group

Syntax

bgp update-group

no bgp update-group

Description

Enables the update-group feature. Update-group is used to optimize BGP processing. With update-group
enabled, neighbors that share same outbound policy are grouped under single update-group and update
messages are shared to all peers within that group. Disabled by default.

On enabling/disabling update-group, all the current BGP sessions including dynamic peer sessions will be
restarted. The dynamic peer sessions will get re-established based on the connect-retry timer configured on
the peer device.

BGP determines the AS number size to be used with each individual peer based on the configured AS
octet-size on the peers. If BGP is configured to use 2 octet ASN with peer A and 4 octet ASN with peer B,
then BGP updates sends to peer A and peer B will be different. Therefore, they will form into two different
update-groups.

This feature is supported for IBGP alone. It is not recommended to enable update-group on VPNv4 address
families or configure update-group if an VPNv4 address family is configured. Update-group indices are
created automatically and cannot be configured. Indices are ephemeral in nature. Update-group indices
will not be retained across process restart. When update-group is configured, route reflector clients and
non route reflector clients are parted into different update-groups. The use of update-groups imposes the
following limitations in BGP processing:

1.  While advertising routes to peers of the update-group, the check on route's next-hop is ignored. This
means that, BGP routes can get advertised to a peer even if the next-hop IP of the routes is same as
peer's IP, and the peer should discard such routes.

2.  The use of ORF is restricted. BGP will continue to send outbound route filters to peers, but received

filters are ignored.

3.  Sender loop detection is delayed until the point where UPDATE messages are sent to the members of
the update-group. This means route dropped by the sender due to loop detection will still count as
advertised, as they have been advertised to the update-groups.

It is recommended to enable BGP update-group globally before configuring BGP neighbors. This ensures
that toggling of BGP neighborship can be avoided and memory of the routing process can be optimized.

The no form of this command disables the update-group feature.

Examples

Enabling BGP update-group:

Public

bgp update-group 701

switch(config-bgp)# bgp update-group

All current BGP sessions in VRF default will be restarted.

 Do you want to continue (y/n)?
Disabling BGP update-group:

switch(config-bgp)# no bgp update-group

All current BGP sessions in VRF default will be restarted.

Do you want to continue (y/n)?

clear bgp

Syntax

clear bgp [vrf <VRF-NAME>][ip unicast | ipv6 unicast | l2vpn evpn | all]

     {* | <NEIGHBOR-IP-ADDRESS> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME> } [soft {in | out} ]

Description

Clears BGP with a hard or soft reset. If the address-family is specified, then the neighbors that negotiated
the respective family will be reset or a route refresh message will be sent.

A route refresh message is sent when soft in or soft out is specified.

Parameter

ip unicast

ipv6 unicast

l2vpn evpn

vrf <VRF‐NAME>

Description

Specifies the IPv4 address family.

Specifies the IPv6 address family.

Selects the L2VPN EVPN address family

Specifies a VRF name. By default, the default BGP instance infor
mation is displayed if the VRF is not specified.

all

Specifies all VRFs and address families.

* |  <NEIGHBOR‐IP‐ADDRESS>
| <PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies a neighbor IP address or peer group name for which
peer sessions are to be reset, or  *  to reset all sessions.

soft in

soft out

Examples

Clears the BGP in the inbound direction without a hard reset of
the session. This will send a route refresh request message.

Clears the BGP in the outbound direction without a hard reset o
f the session. This will send a route refresh message.

switch(config)# clear bgp all *

switch# clear bgp all PEER_GRP_1 soft in

switch# clear bgp all PEER_GRP_1 soft out

switch# clear bgp ip unicast 9.0.0.2 soft in
switch# clear bgp ip unicast 9.0.0.2 soft out

switch# clear bgp ip unicast PEER_GRP_1 soft in

switch# clear bgp ip unicast PEER_GRP_1 soft out

Public

clear bgp 703

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 ip unicast 9.0.0.2 soft in

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 ip unicast 9.0.0.2 soft out

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 ip unicast PEER_GRP_1 soft in

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 ip unicast PEER_GRP_1 soft out

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 all PEER_GRP_1 soft in

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 all PEER_GRP_1 soft out

switch# clear bgp ipv6 unicast 9000::2 soft in

switch# clear bgp ipv6 unicast 9000::2 soft out

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 ipv6 unicast 9000::2 soft in

switch# clear bgp vrf v1 ipv6 unicast 9000::2 soft out

switch# clear bgp l2vpn evpn * soft in

switch# clear bgp l2vpn evpn * soft out

switch# clear bgp l2vpn evpn 9.0.0.2 soft in

switch# clear bgp l2vpn evpn 9.0.0.2 soft out

switch# clear bgp l2vpn evpn PEER_GRP_1 soft in

switch# clear bgp l2vpn evpn PEER_GRP_1 soft out

disable|enable

Syntax

disable | enable

Description

This command disables or enables the BGP instance while retaining the configuration. Disable and enable of
the BGP instance may result in a change of the router ID.

By default the BGP instance is enabled.

Examples

switch(config)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp)# disable

NOTE

The routes learned from a peer will not be removed immediately from the BGP's
local RIB when the BGP session on the peer device is disabled. The peer routes
experience a graceful restart.

maximum-paths

Syntax

maximum-paths <MAXPATHS>

no maximum-paths <MAXPATHS>

Description

Configures the maximum number of paths that BGP adds to the route table for equal-cost multipath (ECMP)
load balancing for routes learned from both internal and external BGP. Any changes in BGP configuration are
applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs.

On HPE Aruba Networking 8325, 8325H, 8325P and 10000 switch series, a maximum-paths configuration
is supported globally as well as for ipv4-unicast, ipv6-unicast and l2vpn-evpn address families. For
HPE Aruba Networking 8325, 8325H, and 10000 switch series, this configuration is supported under the
l2vpn-evpn for L2/L3 Overlay ECMP feature. On HPE Aruba Networking 6300, 6400, 8100 and 8360
switch series, this command is supported under the l2vpn-evpn for L2 Overlay ECMP feature.

If address-family specific maximum-paths are configured, they take precedence over global configuration.
Greater than eight maximum-paths at can be configured within the global context, but for l2vpn-evpn
routes the supported maximum limit is still eight.

The no form of this command restores the default setting of 4.

NOTE
For VxLAN L2 Overlay ECMP: On HPE Aruba Networking 6300, 6400, 8100,
8325, 8360 and 10000, this command also controls the maximum number of
paths that BGP adds to the mac-table for equal-cost multipath (ECMP) load
balancing for VxLAN overlay mac routes, reachable via VXLAN tunnels.

Public

maximum-paths 708

Parameter

Description

Specifies the maximum number of paths. 1 to 32 paths can be s
et globally. 1 to 8 paths can be set for l2vpn‐evpn address fa
mily.1 to 32 paths can be set for ipv4‐unicast and ipv6‐uni
cast.

<MAXPATHS>

Usage

When both global and address-family maximum paths are configured then the address family value takes
precedence.

Global max‐path

l2evpn, ipv4‐unicast, or
ipv6‐unicast address‐
family Max‐path

Default( 4)

Default (4)

Configured (4)

configured (4)

Default (4)

8 (max)

32 (max)

8 (max)

Default (8)

configured (4)

Operational Max‐path Comments

4

4

8

8

4

lue for address family pat
hs

The address family value
takes precedence

d to eight paths.

Four is the configured va
lue for address family pat
hs

EVPN Multi-Homing Interoperability

For HPE Aruba Networking 6300 (except 6300L), 6400, 8100, 8325, 8325H, 8360 and 10000 switch
series:

EVPN MAC/IP (Type-2) routes with either non-reserved or reserved ESI values of 0 or MAX-ESI can be
evaluated for forwarding. EVPN MAC/IP routes (Type-2 routes) with a reserved ESI value are resolved
by the MAC/IP route only, using the BGP next-hop within the type-2 route. EVPN MAC/IP routes with a
non-reserved ESI value are resolved only if the route has an associated per-ES Ethernet Auto-Discovery
route (a type-1 route).

6300 (except 6300L), 6400, 8100, 8325, 8325H, 8325P, 8360 and 10000 switch series using EVPN
MAC/IP route resolution with non-reserved ESI values can use VSX multi-homing for locally connected
devices while coexisting in a EVPN-VXLAN fabric with other switches using EVPN multi-homing to connect
local devices. The MAC and IP addresses of remote endpoints are learned from those remote switches using

Public

maximum-paths 709

EVPN control plane messages, and are assigned multiple next-hop IP addresses (unique VTEP addresses
that identify switches using EVPN multi-homing).

Examples

Configuring the maximum number of global paths:

switch(config)# router bgp 1

switch(config-bgp)# maximum-paths 32

All current BGP sessions in VRF default will be restarted.

Do you want to continue (y/n)? y

Configuring the maximum paths for the IPv4-unicast address family:

switch(config-bgp)# address-family ipv4 unicast

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)#  maximum-paths 6
Configuring the maximum-paths paths for the IPv6-unicast address family:

switch(config-bgp)# address-family ipv6 unicast

switch(config-bgp-ipv6-uc)#  maximum-paths 6
Configuring the maximum-paths for the l2vpn-evpn address family:

switch(config-bgp)# address-family l2vpn evpn

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# maximum-paths 6
Removing the global maximum paths setting:

switch(config-bgp)# no maximum-paths

All current BGP sessions in VRF default will be restarted.

Do you want to continue (y/n)? y

NOTE

For more information on features that use this command, refer to the Multicast
Guide for your switch model.

neighbor activate

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} activate
no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>}activate

Public

neighbor activate 711

Description

This command enables the address-family capability and exchange of information specific to an address
family with a BGP neighbor.

The no form of this command removes the address-family capability and disables the exchange of routes for
the specified address-family with the BGP neighbor.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de
cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:x
xxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f
rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the nei
ghbor.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

NOTE

VPNV4 address-family, peer group, and BFD are not supported for BGP
unnumbered.

Examples

Enabling and removing the address-family capability and exchange of information on 1/1/1.

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1/1/1 remote-as 1

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1/1/1 activate

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1/1/1 activate

Enabling and removing the address-family capability and exchange of information on 1/1/1.1.

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1/1/1.1

switch(config-bgp-ipv6-uc)# neighbor 1/1/1.1 activate

switch(config-bgp-ipv6-uc)# no neighbor 1/1/1.1 activate

Enabling and removing the address-family capability and exchange of information on 1/1/1:1.

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1/1/1:1
switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1/1/1:1 activate

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1/1/1:1 activate

Enabling and removing the address-family capability and exchange of information on 1.1.1.1.

Public

neighbor activate 712

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 activate

neighbor advertisement-interval

Public

neighbor advertisement-interval 713

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} advertisement-interval

<INTERVAL>

no neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} advertisement-interval

Description

Sets the advertisement interval, which defines the length of time between transmission of BGP routing
updates.

The no form of this command restores the default value. Default values are 30 seconds for external BGP
peer and 5 seconds for internal BGP peer.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de
cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:x
xxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f
rom 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a Peer‐Group. This is not supported on BGP unnum
bered.

Specifies the advertisement interval in seconds. Range: 0 to 60
0. Default: 30 for external BGP peer and 5 for internal BGP peer.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<INTERVAL>

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 advertisement-interval 20

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 advertisement-interval

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor pg advertisement-interval 50

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor pg advertisement-interval

neighbor add-paths

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths {send | recv |
both}

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths {send | recv |

both}

Public

neighbor add-paths 715

Description

Enables additional path capability for BGP as described in RFC 7911. This allows BGP peer to send, receive,
or send and receive multiple paths for the same address prefix without the subsequent advertisements
implicitly replacing any previous paths. The additional path includes the first (N-1) best paths, which means
that the total paths for an address prefix received by a BGP speaker will include the best path and the
additional paths determined by its BGP peer.

With additional path feature, each path is identified by a path identifier in addition to the address prefix. To
use this command, the backup path of BGP next-hop must be different than the primary path.

The no form of this command disables the additional path feature.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

add‐paths {send | recv |
both}

Configures the additional paths in one of the following ways:

•  send —Enables the neighbor to send the additional path

s.

•  recv —Enables the neighbor to receive the additional p

aths.

•  both —Enables the neighbor to send and receive the add

itional paths.

Examples

Enabling BGP neighbor to send the additional paths:

switch(config)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp)# address-family ipv4 unicast

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 add-paths send

Public

neighbor add-paths 716

Disabling BGP neighbor to send the additional paths:

switch(config)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp)# address-family ipv4 unicast

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 add-paths send

neighbor add-paths advertise-best

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths advertise-best

<2-4>

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths advertise-best

<2-4>

Description

Controls the number of best BGP Paths to be advertised by a BGP speaker to a BGP peer. When enabled, it
allows BGP speaker to advertise more than one best paths for the same address prefix. The total paths for
an address prefix will include the best path and the additional paths.

The no form of this command removes the advertise best path configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

advertise‐best <2‐4>

Specifies the number of best BGP paths to be advertised to a
BGP Peer. Range: 2 to 4. Default: 2.

Examples

Setting the number of best paths to send to the neighbor:

switch(config)# router bgp 100
switch(config-bgp)# address-family ipv4 unicast
switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 add-paths advertise-best 3

Removing the advertise best path configuration:

Public

neighbor add-paths advertise-best 718

switch(config)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp)# address-family ipv4 unicast

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 add-paths advertise-best 3

neighbor allowas-in

Public

neighbor allowas-in 719

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<LIMIT>

no neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<LIMIT>

Description

Specifies the number of times that the AS path of a received route can contain the AS number of the
recipient BGP speaker and still be accepted. When this configuration is applied to a peer-group, all the
neighbors that are part of the peer-group inherit this setting.

The no form of this command restores the default setting, which is to reject as a loop any route where the
path contains the speaker AS number.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDRESS>

Specifies the neighbor IP address in the IPv4 format (x.x.x.x),
where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or in the IPv6 form
at (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a
hexadecimal number from 0 to F.

NOTE
IPv6 MP‐BGP peering must not be used for L
2VPN EVPN address family, because VXLAN tu
nnel interface does not support IPv6 addresses.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group.

Specifies the number of times that the AS path of a received ro
ute can contain the AS number of the recipient BGP. Range: 1 to
10.

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<LIMIT>

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 allowas-in 5

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 allowas-in

switch(config-bgp-ipv6-uc)# neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334 allowas-

Public

neighbor allowas-in 720

in 5

switch(config-bgp-ipv6-uc)# no neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

allowas-in

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor PG allowas-in 5

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor PG allowas-in

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 allowas-in 5

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 allowas-in

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor PG allowas-in 5

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor PG allowas-in

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

allowas-in 5

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

neighbor ao

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ao <keychain-name>

   accept-ao-mismatch

   include-tcp-options

   no ...

Description

Enables TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) authentication on a TCP connection between two BGP
neighbors. To disable this function, use the no form of this command.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

<keychain‐name>

Name of the keychain for the neighbor.

The keychain allows keys to be configured with different valid li
fetimes.

This mechanism provides a way for a set of keys to be rotated a
nd hence protect against long‐lived‐key attacks. At any give
n time only one key is selected as active‐key and keys are vali
d for a duration of the defined send‐lifetime. If the send‐lifet

Public

neighbor ao 722

Parameter

Description
ime and the accept‐lifetimes are not configured for the key, th
e key is considered to be valid for infinite lifetime.

When multiple keys are configured, its recommended that keys
overlap in their send‐lifetimes so that the key rollover occurs
at the start of the next key's send‐lifetime. This allows for a co
ntinuous key usage by TCP‐AO.

 accept‐ao‐mismatch

Accept incoming TCP segments without TCP‐AO option.

If enabled, the device will accept a connection from the peer
even if the received TCP packets do not contain the TCP‐AO o
ption.

Include the TCP header options for MAC calculation. Note that e
nabling this setting will immediately reset the neighbor session.
This setting is disabled by default.

Negates any configured parameter.

include‐tcp‐options

no ...

Usage

TCP-AO authentication can not be used with the neighbor password feature. When TCP-AO is applied
to a peer-group, all the neighbors in peer-group will inherit the peer-group configuration unless there
is a configuration specific to an individual neighbor. If a peer-group is configured with the neighbor
password feature but the neighbors that belong to that peer-group are configured with TCP-AO, the TCP-AO
configuration will be rejected. Similarly, If a peer-group is configured to use TCP-AO authentication, the
neighbors that belong to that peer-group will reject the neighbor password.

The TCP-AO feature takes a keychain as a parameter. The key will not be valid until a Recv-D, Send-ID, and
send lifetime is configured. The supported cryptographic algorithms for TCP-AO are:

•  HMAC-SHA-1-96 based on [RFC2104] and [FIPS-180-3]

•  AES-128-CMAC-96 based on [NIST-SP800-38B][FIPS197]

Examples

switch(config)# keychain bgpkeys

switch(config-keychain)# key 1

switch(config-keychain-key)# send-lifetime start-time 10:10:10 10/25/2022

duration infinite

switch(config-keychain-key)# accept-lifetime start-time 10:10:10 10/25/2022

duration infinite
switch(config-keychain-key)# send-id 10

switch(config-keychain-key)# recv-id 10

switch(config-keychain-key)# cryptographic-algorithm aes-cmac-128

switch(config-keychain-key)# key-string plaintext qwer

Public

neighbor ao 723

switch(config)# router bgp 1

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 ao bgpkeys

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 ao accept-ao-mismatch

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 ao include-tcp-option

neighbor capability orf prefix-list

Public

neighbor capability orf prefix-list 724

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>} capability orf prefix-list {send | receive

| both}

no neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>} capability orf prefix-list {send |

receive | both}

Description

Enables the Outbound Route filtering (ORF) capability with the neighbor in one of the three available
modes. The available modes are send, receive, and both. The ORF capability is executed based on prefix list
only.

The Outbound Route Filtering (ORF) capability provides a mechanism for a BGP speaker to send a set of
Outbound Route Filters (ORFs) that can be used by its BGP peer to filter its outbound routing updates to the
speaker. This is a filtering method used to reduce the computation on the router receiving the route.

The no form of this command disables the ORF capability.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

capability orf prefix‐list
  {send | receive | both}

Enables ORF prefix list capability with the neighbor in one of th
e following modes:

•  send ‐ Enables the ORF prefix list capability in send mode

.

•  receive ‐ Enables the ORF prefix list capability in receive

mode.

•  both‐ Enables the ORF prefix list capability in both send

and receive mode.

Examples

Enabling the ORF prefix list capability in both send and receive mode:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 capability orf prefix-list both

Enabling the ORF prefix list capability in send mode:

Public

neighbor capability orf prefix-list 725

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 capability orf prefix-list send

Disabling the ORF prefix list capability in both send and receive mode: :

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 capability orf prefix-list

both

neighbor default-originate

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} default-originate [route-

map <MAP-NAME>]

no neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} default-originate

[route-map <MAP-NAME>]

Description

Enables the local router to send the default route 0.0.0.0 to a neighbor. The neighbor can then use this
route to reach the router when all other routes are unavailable. Use the  route-map  option to configure
the route map to modify the default route attributes.

The no form of this command disables this feature.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de
cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:x
xxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f
rom 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Sets the route map to modify the default route attributes.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<MAP‐NAME>

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 default-originate

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 default-originate
switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor PG default-originate

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor PG default-originate

Public

neighbor default-originate 727

neighbor ebgp-multihop

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ebgp-multihop <ttl>
no neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ebgp-multihop <ttl>

Public

neighbor ebgp-multihop 728

Description

Enables BGP to establish a session with external peers residing on networks that are not directly connected.
By default, BGP can only establish sessions with external BGP peers that are directly connected. If external
BGP peer are not connected directly, then ebgp-multihop is enabled to initiate the connection with that
external peer. If ebgp-multihop is disabled and external BGP peers are indirectly connected, then BGP peer
session is not established.

The no form of this command disables the peer ebgp-multihop feature.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<ttl>

Examples

Specifies a peer group.

Specifies the ttl value.

Enabling BGP to establish connection with external peers residing on networks that are not directly
connected:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 ebgp-multihop 5

Disabling BGP to establish connection with external peers residing on networks that are not directly
connected:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 ebgp-multihop

Enabling BGP to establish connection with external peers residing on networks that are not directly
connected:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg ebgp-multihop 5

Disabling BGP to establish connection with external peers residing on networks that are not directly
connected:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg ebgp-multihop 5

Public

neighbor ebgp-multihop 729

neighbor fall-over

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over
no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over

Public

neighbor fall-over 730

Description

Enables BGP fast peering session deactivation. When neighbor fall-over is configured, the BGP process
monitors the RIB and if the route to peer is not present in the routing table, it immediately deactivates the
peer session without waiting for the hold down timer. It is disabled by default.

The no form of this command disables this feature.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Usage

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Neighbor fall-over does not track connected or static routes to peers. However, this is not an issue when
IBGP peering is using a loopback interface. To force a fall-over for connected and static routes, use the
command  neighbor fall-over bfd .

Examples

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 fall-over

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 fall-over

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor PG fall-over

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor PG fall-over

neighbor fall-over bfd

Syntax

neighbor {<IPV4-ADDR>|<IPV6-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over bfd
no neighbor {<IPV4-ADDR>|<IPV6-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over bfd

Description

Enables BGP to register with BFD to receive fast peering session deactivation messages from BFD. You can
either configure BFD support for BGP per neighbor or peer-group.

The no form of this command disables BGP for BFD.

NOTE
Multihop BFD is not supported for BGP.

Public

neighbor fall-over bfd 732

Parameter

Description

Specifies the neighbor address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x
is a decimal number from 0 to 255.

Specifies the neighbor address in IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx
:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal numbe
r from 0 to F. This parameter applies only to HPE Aruba Networ
king 6300 and 6400 Switch series.

Specifies a peer group.

<IPV4‐ADDR>

<IPV6‐ADDR>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Examples

Enabling BFD for BGP neighbor with IPv4 address:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 fall-over bfd

Enabling BFD for BGP neighbor with IPv6 address (applies only to HPE Aruba Networking 6300 and 6400
Switch series):

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1000::1 fall-over bfd

Enabling BFD for peer group:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor PG fall-over bfd

Disabling BFD for BGP per neighbor IPv4 address:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 fall-over bfd

Disabling BFD for BGP per neighbor with IPv6 address (applies only to HPE Aruba Networking 6300 and
6400 Switch series):

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1000::1 fall-over bfd

Disabling BFD for peer group:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor PG fall-over bfd

Public

neighbor fall-over bfd 733

neighbor graceful-shutdown

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown

     [local-preference <LOCAL-PREF>
     | <CONFIG-DELAY>

    |<LOCAL-PREF> ]

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown

     [local-preference <LOCAL-PREF>

Public

neighbor graceful-shutdown 734

| <CONFIG-DELAY>

     |<LOCAL-PREF> ]

Description

Configures the wait time before shutting down the BGP neighbor session, and can also configure the local
preference value to be advertised before graceful shutdown.

The no form of this command sets the wait time to the default value of 180 seconds and the local-
preference value to the default of 0.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

local‐preference<LOCAL‐
PREF>

<CONFIG‐DELAY>

Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de
cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:x
xxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f
rom 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies the local preference value for exporting the iBGP rou
tes during a graceful shutdown. The lower the value, the lower t
he local preference. A value of 0 indicates that the route is the l
east preferred. Range: 0 to 4294967295. Default: 0.

Specifies the time to wait before shutting down the neighbor in
seconds. Range: 10 to 1200. Default: 180.

Public

neighbor graceful-shutdown 735

Usage

If the graceful shutdown timer has already started and the administrator configures a command that triggers
a session restart, traffic loss can occur if the graceful shutdown delay is not sufficient for the BGP peers to
converge to a new route.

NOTE

On each Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR) supporting the graceful
shutdown receiver procedure, an inbound BGP route policy must be applied on all
EBGP sessions of the ASBR.
The policy must match the GSHUT community and lower the precedence of the
route by changing the route attributes.

The Graceful-Shutdown feature does not work for reflected routes because the route reflector (RR) does not
modify local-preference attribute. The routes, originated by the RR, carry the GSHUT local-preference value.
As per the RFC 4456, when an RR reflects a route, it should not modify the following path attributes:

•  NEXT-HOP

•  AS-PATH

•  LOCAL-PREF

•  MED

Their modification could potentially result in routing loops. In this situation, apply on the RR an inbound BGP
route policy, meeting the following conditions:

•  Match the graceful-shutdown community.

•  Set the local preference attributes of the paths tagged with the graceful-shutdown community to a

lower value than other routes to the same destination.

Examples

Setting the wait time delay:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown 10

Setting the local-preference value:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown local-preference 100

Setting the wait time delay and local-preference value:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown 10 local-preference
100

Setting the wait time delay to the default of 180 seconds:

Public

neighbor graceful-shutdown 736

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown 10

Setting the local-preference value to default of 0:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown local-preference

100

Setting the wait time delay and local-preference value to defaults:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown 10 local-

preference 100

Complete deletion:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown

neighbor interface

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} interface <ifname>

[no] neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} interface <ifname>

Description

Configure a BGP neighbor by specifying the interface name on which the neighbor is connected instead of
the neighbor’s IP address. When an unnumbered interface is specified to the neighbor command, a static
route is auto-provisioned for the neighbor with the unnumbered interface as a destination. A static route is
also auto-provisioned if the neighbor IP address is learned via LLDP.

NOTE

If OSPF is enabled on an unnumbered interface then the auto-provisioning is not
supported as the route to the BGP peer is expected to be learned via OSPF.

Peer-group is not supported for BGP peering on the IP unnumbered interface.

The no form of this command disables this feature.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

Examples

Configuring unnumbered BGP peering on interface 1/1/1:

switch(config)# router bgp 100
switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 100

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 interface 1/1/1

Disabling this feature:

Public

neighbor interface 738

switch(config)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 interface 1/1/1

neighbor listen ip-range

Syntax

neighbor <PEER-GROUP-NAME> listen ip-range <IP-ADDR>|<MASK> {as-range <AS-

RANGE>|limit <LIMIT>}

Public

neighbor listen ip-range 739

no neighbor <PEER-GROUP-NAME> listen ip-range <IP-ADDR>|

            <MASK> {as-range <AS-RANGE>|limit <LIMIT>}

Description

Configures BGP dynamic neighbors as ranges of remote addresses with associated peer groups.

The no form of this command restores default behavior.

NOTE

•  Each range of remote addresses is configured as a remote address prefix.

•  Any BGP peer with a remote address that matches the remote address prefix

becomes a member of the associated peer group.

Parameter

Description

Specifies peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbere
d.

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies subnet range.

<IP‐ADDR>

|<MASK>

<AS‐RANGE>

<LIMIT>

Restrictions

Specifies AS number as a range in integer or dotted format.

Specifies maximum number of peers. Range: 1 to 256.

•  Dynamic peers are always passive. Outbound connections to dynamic peers are not supported.

•  Dynamic BGP peering is only compatible with peer-groups

Public

neighbor listen ip-range 740

•  Disabling partial AS range is not supported. The exact value that is configured must be used.

◦  When disabling AS range, CLI must use the same AS range that was used when first configured.
For example, if AS range "1-4" is configured, when disabling, "1-4" must be used ("1,2,3,4" is not
supported).

CAUTION

Configuring overlapping peer ranges with different remote address prefix lengths
is not recommended. Peer range configuration is recommended when peer
ranges do not overlap.

Usage

•  All supported address-families are activated on a dynamic peer for negotiation by default.

•

If an incoming connection matches multiple peer range entries, the entry with the longest remote
address prefix is selected.

•  AS ranges are used to match remote AS presented by connecting peers. Remote AS matching with ASes

or AS ranges in this list will be accepted.

◦  AS range only applies to dynamic peers.

•  The limit option is used to set the maximum number of dynamic BGP peers within the peer range. The

default is 512 if no limit is set.

If the limit is reached, BGP rejects incoming connections from new dynamic BGP peers until BGP
session termination causes the number of dynamic BGP peers to fall below the limit.

If the limit is reduced below the current number of dynamic BGP peers, BGP will reject incoming
connections from new dynamic BGP peers until the number of dynamic BGP peers falls below the
new limit. BGP will not terminate existing BGP sessions with dynamic BGP peers in this case. If an
existing BGP session gets terminated, that session will not re-establish until the number of BGP
sessions falls below the limit.

◦

◦

•

•  After dynamic peer is configured, additional configuration is required on the peer-group as a whole.
Individual member groups are incompatible. For example, the neighbor shutdown command can be
executed on a peer-group, but not on individual members of the peer-group.

•  When a peer is configured as dynamic and is in an established state, a shutdown is required before

reconfiguring as static.

•  Connect-retry interval is recommended to be configured with a smaller value than the default value on

the active peer.

Public

neighbor listen ip-range 741

•  When a set of valid and invalid AS values are issued (separated by commas), only the valid values are

accepted.

•

•

•  When the AS range parameter is not explicitly configured in dynamic bgp peering, iBGP session comes

up, eBGP session does not. If there are no configured remote AS or AS list entries, DC-BGP assumes that
any peer is an iBGP peer.

Examples

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg listen ip-range 192.168.0.0/16

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg listen ip-range 192.168.0.0/16

neighbor local-as

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} local-as <AS-NUMBER> [no-

prepend[replace-as[dual-as]]]

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} local-as [no-

prepend[replace-as[dual-as]]]

Description

Configures an alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer, allowing a
router to appear to be a member of a second autonomous system (AS), and its real AS.

Local AS allows two autonomous systems to merge without modifying peering arrangements. This command
is valid only for external peers.

The no form of this command restores the default, which is for a peering session to be established using the
primary AS (primary AS is the AS number specified at the time of neighbor creation using the command  ne
ighbor remote-as ).

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

local‐as <AS‐NUMBER>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies a 4‐byte AS number in asplain format (z), or asdot
format (x.y), where z is a number from 1 to 4294967295 and x
and y are 16‐bit numbers.

Public

neighbor local-as 743

Parameter

no‐prepend

replace‐as

dual‐as

Examples

Description

Optional. When you include this parameter, the switch will not p
repend local ASN value to the AS path list for all the routes rece
ived from the peer.

Optional. Replaces the configured router AS with the local AS in
the EBGP updates.

Optional. Use this option to form the neighborship with any of t
he two AS numbers; the configured router ASN or the local ASN
.

Configuring an alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 local-as 200

Removing the alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 local-as 200

Configuring an alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg local-as 200 no-prepend

Removing the alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg local-as 200 no-prepend

Configuring an alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg local-as 200 no-prepend replace-as

Removing the alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg local-as 200 no-prepend replace-as

Configuring an alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg local-as 200 no-prepend replace-as dual-as

Removing the alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg local-as 200 no-prepend replace-as dual-
as

Public

neighbor local-as 744

neighbor maximum-prefix

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}
        maximum-prefix <MAXIMUM> [threshold <THRESHOLD>]

        [restart <INTERVAL>] [warning-only]

no neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} maximum-prefix<MAXIMUM>

Public

neighbor maximum-prefix 745

[threshold <THRESHOLD>]

        [restart <INTERVAL>] [warning-only]

Description

Sets the maximum number of prefixes that can be received from a neighbor.

The session is re-established only if the number of routes received from the BGP peer does not exceed the
configured prefix limit. When the restart timer is configured, sessions are automatically re-established when
the timer expires. By default, the device accepts 128,000 prefixes from a BGP neighbor with a threshold
value of 75%. A warning message is generated when the number of prefixes per neighbor reaches 75%
of default prefix limit. Another warning message is generated when the default prefix limit is reached. A
maximum prefix limit applies at the neighbor level. If maximum-prefix limit exceeds any configured limit, the
BGP session with that neighbor will be brought down.

The no form of this command disables the maximum number of prefixes limit.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the IP address of the neighbor in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x)
, where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xx
xx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexad
ecimal number from 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a Peer‐Group. This is not supported on BGP unnum
bered.

 maximum‐prefix <MAXIMUM>

Specifies the maximum number of prefixes allowed from the sp
ecified neighbor. Range: 1 to 128000. Default: 128000.

threshold <THRESHOLD>

Specifies at what percentage of  MAXIMUM  a warning messag
e is generated. Range: 1 to 100. Default: 75.

For example, if  MAXIMUM  is set to 1000 and threshold is 70, t
he router generates a warning message when the number of BG
P learned routes from the neighbor exceeds 70 percent of 100
0 (700) routes.

restart <INTERVAL>

Specifies interval in seconds for restarting the BGP connection
when the prefix limit is exceeded. Range: 30 to 65535.

Public

neighbor maximum-prefix 746

Parameter

warning‐only

Description

Specifies generating and logging a warning message without di
sconnecting the BGP session when the prefix limit is exceeded.

Examples

Setting the prefix limit to 1000 prefixes:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 10.0.0.1 maximum-prefix 1000

Enabling logging of a warning message when more than 1000 prefixes are received:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 10.0.0.1 maximum-prefix 1000 warning-

only

Setting the prefix limit to 1000 prefixes and enabling logging of a warning message when 500 prefixes are
received:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 10.0.0.1 maximum-prefix 1000 threshold

50

Setting the prefix limit to 1000 prefixes and enabling logging of a warning message when 500 prefixes are
received and a second warning when the prefix limit is exceeded without disconnecting the session:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 10.0.0.1 maximum-prefix 1000 threshold

50 warning-only

Removing the threshold value:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 10.0.0.1 maximum-prefix 1000

threshold 50

Disabling the maximum-prefix feature:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 10.0.0.1 maximum-prefix

neighbor next-hop-self

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}next-hop-self
no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} next-hop-self

Description

Configures the router as the next hop for a BGP-speaking neighbor or peer group, and enables BGP to send
itself as the next hop for advertised routes.

The no form of this command resets the peer next-hop-self status to default. The next hop is generated
based on the IP.

Public

neighbor next-hop-self 748

Parameter

Description

Specifies the neighbor's IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), wh
ere x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx
:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadeci
mal number from 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Applies the configuration to all route‐reflector clients.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

all

Usage

•  An administrator uses this command to make a BGP speaker fill its address when advertising routes to a

BGP peer.

•  This command is useful in non-meshed networks where BGP neighbors may not have direct access to all

other neighbors on the same IP subnet.

•  While advertising routes to eBGP peers, the next-hop is set to self IP by default. The default behavior

can be changed by configuring next-hop-unchanged.

•  While advertising routes to iBGP peers, the next-hop is kept unchanged by default. The default behavior

can be changed by configuring next-hop-self.

BGP L2VPN EVPN context:

•  The next-hop-self command under l2vpn-evpn address-family, in addition to replacing the next-hop IP
of EVPN routes with self IP, replaces the routers-mac extended community of EVPN routes with self
MAC.

Examples

BGP IPv4 UC context:

Setting and resetting the router as the next hop self for neighbor 1.1.1.1:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 next-hop-self

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 next-hop-self

Public

neighbor next-hop-self 749

Setting and resetting the router as the next hop self for its peer group:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor pg next-hop-self

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor pg next-hop-self

BGP L2VPN EVPN context:

NOTE

Supported only on the HPE Aruba Networking 8325 Switch Series

Setting and resetting the router as the next hop self for neighbor 1.1.1.1:

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 next-hop-self

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 next-hop-self

Setting and resetting the router as the next hop self for its peer group:

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor pg next-hop-self

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor pg next-hop-self

neighbor next-hop-unchanged

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>} next-hop-unchanged

no neighbor  {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>} next-hop-unchanged

Description

Enables the neighbor to preserve next-hop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN
address-family.

The no form of this command resets the peer next-hop-unchanged status to default.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDRESS>

Specifies the neighbor IP address in the IPv4 format (x.x.x.x),
where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or in the IPv6 form
at (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a
hexadecimal number from 0 to F.

NOTE
IPv6 MP‐BGP peering must not be used for L
2VPN EVPN address family, because VXLAN tu
nnel interface does not support IPv6 addresses.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

<IFNAME>

Public

neighbor next-hop-unchanged 751

Examples

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 next-hop-unchanged

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334 next-

hop-unchanged

The next-hop-unchanged option needs to be explicitly configured to preserve the nexthop while
advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. For example:

   router bgp 1

neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 2

      address-family l2vpn evpn

         neighbor 1.1.1.1 activate

neighbor 1.1.1.1 next-hop-unchanged

         neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community extended

      exit-address-family

neighbor orf prefix-list in

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>} orf-prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> in

no neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>} orf-prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> in

Description

Applies an inbound prefix list filter to filter the distribution of BGP neighbor information.

The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not applying the prefix list filter.

NOTE

This command must be used only along with the ORF capability to take effect.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

orf‐prefix‐list PREFIX‐
LIST‐NAME>

Sends the prefix list name to be filtered.

Usage

To use this command, the following conditions must be met:

Public

neighbor orf prefix-list in 753

•

•

If route-map inbound is also applied on multiple neighbors along with ORF, then the route-map name
must be common on all the neighbors.

If route-map inbound is also applied on an IPv6 AF BGP neighbor, then the route-map sequence number
with value 1 cannot be used.

Examples

Applying the inbound prefix list filter:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 orf-prefix-list ABC in

Removing the inbound prefix list filter:

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 orf-prefix-list ABC in

neighbor passive

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}  passive

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} passive

Description

Configures a specific neighbor, or all neighbors in a peer-group, as passive, which means that they will not
initiate the TCP session.

The no form of this command enables the neighbor to initiate the TCP session.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group.

Examples

Configure a specific neighbor as passive:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 passive

Enabling the local router to initiate the TCP session for a specified neighbor:

Public

neighbor passive 755

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 passive

Configure a specific peer group as passive:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg passive

Enabling the local router to initiate the TCP session for a specified peer group:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg passive

neighbor password

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}      password [{ciphertext

| plaintext} <PASSWORD>]

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}

            password [ciphertext <PASSWORD>]

Description

Enables message digest5 (MD5) authentication on a TCP connection between two BGP neighbors. When
the password is applied to a peer-group, all the neighbors that are part of peer-group inherit the configured
setting.

The no form of this command removes the neighbor password.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a Peer‐Group. This is not supported on BGP unnum
bered.

{ciphertext | plaintext}

Selects the password format.

Specifies the password.

<PASSWORD>

NOTE
When the password is not provided on the command line, plaintext password
prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered password characters are
masked with asterisks.

Public

neighbor password 757

Examples

Enabling message digest5 (MD5) authentication for a neighbor with a provided plaintext password:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 password plaintext doubt_Plane#93

Enabling message digest5 (MD5) authentication for a neighbor with a prompted plaintext password:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.5 password

Enter the neighbor password: *************

Re-Enter the neighbor password: *************

Enabling message digest5 (MD5) authentication for a peer group with a provided plaintext password:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg_3 password plaintext doubt_Plane#93

Disabling message digest5 (MD5) authentication for a neighbor:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.5 password

Disabling message digest5 (MD5) authentication for a peer group:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg_3 password

neighbor port

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} port <NUMBER>

no neighbor{<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} port <NUMBER>

Description

Sets a custom TCP port on which to communicate with the BGP neighbor.

When this configuration is applied for peer-group, all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will inherit
this setting. Though the neighbor inherits the configuration from the peer-group, the neighbor-specific
command, if configured, takes precedence.

This setting only takes effect after a hard reset of the session.

The no form of this command allows a random TCP port to be selected for the communication with the BGP
neighbor.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Public

neighbor port 759

Parameter

Description

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

port <NUMBER>

Specifies a TCP port number. Range: 0 to 65535.

Examples

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 port 1500

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 port

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor PG port 1500

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor PG port

neighbor remote-as

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-NUMBER>

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-NUMBER>

Description

Creates a peer, initiates the connection to the peer, and adds an entry to the BGP neighbor table. Specifies
a neighbor with an autonomous system (AS) number that identifies the neighbor as internal to the local
autonomous system. Otherwise, the neighbor is considered as external. By default, neighbors that are
defined using this command, exchange only unicast address prefixes.

The no form of this command disables the peer session and deletes the peer information.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

remote‐as <AS‐NUMBER>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies a 4‐byte AS number in asplain format (z), or asdot
format (x.y), where z is a number from 1 to 4294967295 and x
and y are 16‐bit numbers in the range 0 to 65535.

Public

neighbor remote-as 761

Usage

The configured peer AS number is compared with the AS number received in the open message and a peer
session is initiated only if both the AS numbers match.

Examples

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 1

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 1

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg remote-as 1

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg remote-as 1

neighbor remote-as 762

neighbor remote as

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-no>

no neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-no>

Description

Creates an unnumbered peer and initiates the connection to the peer and adds an entry to the BGP neighbor
table. This specifies a neighbor on the interface that is directly connected to the peer. This command
eliminates specifying interface ip and remote-as, making BGP configuration simple and automated. The
remote-as column in the bgp_neighbor table is optional for this type of peer. This command is applicable
only for EBGP peers. If IBGP peers are configured via REST, the configuration is accepted, but the session is
not established.

The no form of the command disables the peer session and deletes the peer information.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

remote as

<AS no>

Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de
cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:x
xxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f
rom 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies the peer group.

Specifies the AS number as an integer value. Range: 1‐42949
67295.

This is optional for BGP unnumbered.

Specifies the As number in dotted format. Range: 0.1‐65535.
65535.

Public

neighbor remote as 763

Usage

This command eliminates specifying interface ip and remote-as, making BGP configuration simple and
automated. The remote-as column in the bgp_neighbor table is optional for this type of peer. This command
is applicable only for EBGP peers. If IBGP peers are configured via REST, the configuration is accepted,
but the session is not established. BGP unnumbered is supported on IPv4, IPv6, and evpn address-family.
no ipv6 nd supress-ra must be configured on the interface that is used for BGP unnumbered. If BGP
unnumbered is configured over an SVI interface, then only the interface is expected to have the router
connected to it. BGP unnumbered peering is not supported on VPNv4 address-family deployments. BGP
unnumbered neighbor statistics are not displayed when the corresponding interface is not IPv6 enabled or is
down. Split and subinterfaces are supported.

Examples

Creating an unnumbered peer:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 1

Disabling the peer session:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 1

Creating an unnumbered peer using IPv6:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1/1/11 remote-as 65013

Disabling the peer session:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1/1/11 remote-as 65013

Creating an unnumbered peer using subinterface:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1/1/27.1 remote-as 65101

Disabling an unnumbered peer using subinterface:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1/1/27.1 remote-as 65101

Creating an unnumbered peer using SVI:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor vlan100 remote-as 65013

Disabling an unnumbered peer using SVI:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor vlan100 remote-as 65013

Creating a peer and configuring the peer group:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg remote-as 1

Disabling the peer session and peer group:

Public

neighbor remote as 764

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg remote-as 1

neighbor remove-private-AS

Syntax

neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remove-private-AS

no neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}  remove-private-AS

Public

neighbor remove-private-AS 765

Description

This command forces the BGP neighbor to drop the private AS numbers. When the outbound update
contains a sequence of private AS numbers, this sequence is dropped. If the command is configured for
peer-group, then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will remove the private-AS before sending the
BGP update message.

The no form of this command allows the private-AS number to be carried in BGP update message.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Examples

Forcing the BGP neighbor to drop the private AS numbers:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 remove-private-AS

Allowing the private AS number to be carried in the BGP update message:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 remove-private-AS

Forcing the BGP neighbor to drop the private AS numbers:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor PG remove-private-AS

Allowing the private AS number to be carried in the BGP update message:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor PG remove-private-AS

Public

neighbor remove-private-AS 766

neighbor replace-rt-asn

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IPv6-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} replace-rt-asn
no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IPv6-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} replace-rt-asn

Public

neighbor replace-rt-asn 767

Description

This command enables the BGP router to replace the ASN portion of the route target in the received BGP
update message with the local AS number. It facilitates usage of auto RT on all BGP peers across EVPN
fabrics.

This command can be used if VNIs are same across fabric and route-target auto is being used. When
replace-rt-asn and a route-map with set extcommunity rt applied in the "IN" direction on a neighbor,
replace-rt-asn will override the 2-byte of extended-community with local-AS.

The bgp connection must be reset using clear bgp to allow this configuration to take effect.

Parameter

Description

Specifies the neighbor's IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), wh
ere x is a decimal number from 0 to 255.

Specifies the neighbor's IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx:xxxx:x
xxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal nu
mber from 0 to F.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported for BGP unnumbe
red.

Restores default behavior.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IPv6‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

no

Examples

Specifying the neighbor's IP address:

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 replace-rt-asn

Specifying a peer group name:

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor PG replace-rt-asn

Public

neighbor replace-rt-asn 768

neighbor route-map

Syntax

neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}  route-map <MAP-NAME> {in|

out}
no neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-map <MAP-NAME>

{in|out}

Public

neighbor route-map 769

Description

This command applies a route map to incoming or outgoing routes. It configures the route map for
modifying the default attributes of the route.

NOTE

When both peer group and neighbor configuration have route maps associated,
then the following configuration applies:

•  For outbound route maps, peer group configuration will override the

configuration of the neighbor.

•  For inbound route maps, neighbor configuration will override the peer group

configuration.

The no form of this command removes a route map.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<MAP‐NAME>

in|out

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies the name of the route map.

Sets the route map policy to apply to either the received routes
from the neighbor ( in ) or the advertised routes to the neigh
bor ( out ).

Public

neighbor route-map 770

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-map HPE in

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-map HPE in

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor PG route-map HPE in

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor PG route-map HPE in

switch(config)# route-map Rmap permit seq 10

switch(config-route-map-Rmap-10)# match metric 100

switch(config-route-map-bgp-10)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 remote-as 100

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-map Rmap out

Configuring inbound route maps in L2VPN EVPN address family.

switch(config)# router bgp 100

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 2.1.1.1 remote-as 100

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 2.1.1.1 update-source loopback 1

switch(config-bgp)# address-family l2vpn evpn

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2.1.1.1 activate

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2.1.1.1 route-map Rmap in

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2.1.1.1 send-community extended

neighbor route-reflector-client

Syntax

neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-reflector-client

no neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-reflector-client

Description

This command configures the router as a BGP route reflector and the specified peer as its client. The no
form of this command disables this function.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDRESS>

Specifies the neighbor IP address in the IPv4 format (x.x.x.x),
where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or in the IPv6 form
at (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a
hexadecimal number from 0 to F.

NOTE
Pv6 MP‐BGP peering must not be used for L2
VPN EVPN address family, because VXLAN tun
nel interface does not support IPv6 addresses.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

<IFNAME>

Public

neighbor route-reflector-client 772

Parameter

Description

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor PG route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor PG route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor PG route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor PG route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

route-reflector-client

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

route-reflector-client

neighbor send-community

Syntax

neighbor   {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} send-community [standard

| extended]

no neighbor   {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} send-community

[standard | extended]

Description

This command allows community values to be sent to a specific neighbor. When this command is configured
for the peer-group, then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will send the community values to the
peers. The parameters  standard  and  extended  send only the respective community numbers. When
the command is issued without either of these parameters, both standard and extended communities will be
sent to the neighbor.

The no form of this command will not allow the neighbor to send community values to the specific neighbors
that are part of peer-group.

Parameter

Description

<IP‐ADDR>

Specifies the neighbor IP address in the IPv4 format (x.x.x.x),
where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or in the IPv6 form
at (xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx:xxxx), where x is a
hexadecimal number from 0 to F.

NOTE

Public

neighbor send-community 774

Parameter

Description

IPv6 MP‐BGP peering must not be used for L
2VPN EVPN address family, because VXLAN tu
nnel interface does not support IPv6 addresses.

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community extended

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor PG send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor PG send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor PG send-community extended

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# no neighbor PG send-community

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community extended

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 send-community

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor PG send-community standard
switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor PG send-community standard

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor PG send-community extended

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor PG send-community

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334 send-

community extended

switch(config-bgp-l2vpn-evpn)# no neighbor 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334

send-community

neighbor send-community 775

neighbor shutdown

Syntax

neighbor   {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} shutdown
no neighbor   {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} shutdown

Public

neighbor shutdown 776

Description

This command disables the peer session, terminates any active session for the specified neighbor or peer
group, and removes all associated routing information. This action can cause the sudden termination of
many peering sessions.

The no form of this command enables the peer session for the specified neighbor.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Usage

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Sessions are gracefully shut down when graceful-shutdown is enabled. Enter the  neighbor gracefu
l-shutdown  command to enable graceful-shutdown. If graceful-shutdown is configured without delay or
local-preference, the default values are used.

Examples

Disable the peer session, terminate any active session for the specified neighbor or peer group, and
removing all associated routing information. Then enable the peer session for the specified neighbor:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 shutdown

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 shutdown

Disable the peer session, terminate any active session for the specified neighbor or peer group, and
removing all associated routing information. Then enable the peer session for the specified neighbor:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg shutdown

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg shutdown

Establish a peer graceful shutdown:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 graceful-shutdown 10

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 shutdown

Public

neighbor shutdown 777

Peer 1.1.1.1 will be shutdown in 10 seconds. Do not modify BGP

configurations until the shutdown process completes.
Enter an unsuccessful peer session because the gshut timer is running:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 shutdown

Peer 1.1.1.1 is being gracefully shutdown. Peer cannot be brought up until

the shutdown process completes.
Enter an successful peer session because the gshut timer is running:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg shutdown

Reset the timer to the default value:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg graceful-shutdown 10

Added support for BGP unnumbered.

Added graceful shutdown examples and configuration.

neighbor slow-peer

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IPV6-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} slow-peer

no neighbor {<IPV4-ADDR>|<IPV6-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} slow-peer

Description

Slow peer configuration moves the peer from its normal update group to a slow update group. Removing
slow peers allows the normal update group to function without being slowed down and enables faster
convergence. Slow peer configuration is applicable only to explicitly configured BGP neighbors. All
configured slow peers will be grouped into a slow update group. This group will converge based on the
slowest group peer. Dynamic detection and splitting isn't supported. Peers can be statically configured as
slow.

The no form of this command removes the slow peer configuration.

Parameter

Description

Specifies a neighbor IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IPV6‐ADDR>

Examples

Specifies a neighbor IPv6 address.

Configuring a neighbor with an IP address of 1.1.1.1 as a slow peer:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 slow-peer

Removing the slow peer configuration for a neighbor with an IP address of 1.1.1.1:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 slow-peer

Configuring a neighbor with an IPv6 address of 2001::2 as a slow peer:

Public

neighbor slow-peer 779

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 2001::2 slow-peer

Removing the slow peer configuration for a neighbor with an IPv6 address of 2001::2:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 2001::2 slow-peer

neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound

Public

neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound 780

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} soft-reconfiguration inbound

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} soft-reconfiguration

inbound

Description

This command enables you to generate inbound updates from a neighbor and change and activate BGP
policies without clearing the BGP session. Changes in BGP policies require the BGP session to be cleared
which can have a large negative impact on network operations.

The no form of this command disables this setting.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

Usage

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

•  To perform inbound soft reconfiguration, the BGP speaker must store all received route updates,

regardless of the current inbound policy.

•  When inbound soft reconfiguration is enabled, the stored updates are processed by the new policy

configuration to create new inbound updates.

Examples

switch(config-bgp-ipv4-uc)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 soft-reconfiguration inbound

Public

neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound 781

neighbor timers

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers <KEEPALIVE>
            <HOLDTIME>

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers <KEEPALIVE>

Public

neighbor timers 782

<HOLDTIME>

Description

This command sets the timers for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group. When the timer is applied to
peer-group then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will inherit the value configured.

The no form of this command clears the timers for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<KEEPALIVE>

<HOLDTIME>

Examples

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies the Keep‐Alive timer value for the neighbor. Default:
60 seconds. Range: 0‐65535.

Specifies the Hold‐timer value. Default: 180 seconds. Range:
0‐65535.

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 timers 120 360

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 timers 120 360

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg timers 120 360

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg timers 120 360

Public

neighbor timers 783

neighbor timers connect-retry

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers connect-retry
<RETRYTIME>

no neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers connect-retry

Public

neighbor timers connect-retry 784

<RETRYTIME>

Description

This command sets the connect-retry timer for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group.

The no form of this command clears the connect-retry timers for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<IFNAME>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<RETRYTIME>

Usage

Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor.

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies the retry timer value.

When the timer is applied to peer-group, then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group inherit the value
configured. A connect-retry timer configured for a specific peer takes precedence over that configured for
the peer group of this peer. In addition, a connect-retry timer configured for a specific peer or peer group
takes precedence over that configured on the global level.

Examples

Setting the connect-retry timer to 50:

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 timers connect-retry 50

Removing the connect-retry timer:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 timers connect-retry 50

Setting the connect-retry timer to 80:

Public

neighbor timers connect-retry 785

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg timers connect-retry 80

Removing the connect-retry timer:

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg timers connect-retry 80

neighbor ttl-security-hops

Public

neighbor ttl-security-hops 786

Syntax

neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ttl-security-hops <HOP-

COUNT>

no neighbor  {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ttl-security-hops <HOP-

COUNT>

Description

This command enables BGP to establish connection with external peers residing on networks that are
not directly connected. By enabling this feature, the received TTL from a BGP peer is compared with the
difference "255 - hop-count". BGP messages coming with a TTL less than this value are not accepted. BGP
peering will not be established if the TTL in the session establishment is received with a lower value. Also, by
enabling this feature the router will send BGP packets with TTL value of 255 to the neighbor. For a neighbor,
either TTL security or  ebgp-multihop  can be configured, not both together. If there are multiple paths
to reach the node, then the hop count should be configured considering the longest route.

The no form of this command disables the peer ttl-security-hop feature.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDR>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<HOP‐COUNT>

Examples

Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe
red.

Specifies the hop count to reach the neighbor for the eBGP ses
sion. Range: 1‐255.

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 ttl-security-hops 10

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 ttl-security-hops

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor pg ttl-security-hops 5

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor pg ttl-security-hops

Public

neighbor ttl-security-hops 787

Command History

Modification

10.07 or earlier

neighbor update-source

Syntax

neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}

     update-source {<IPv4>|<IPv6> | loopback <NUMBER>}
no neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>}

     update-source [<IPv4>|<IPv6> | loopback <NUMBER>]

Public

neighbor update-source 788

Description

This command specifies the source address to reach the neighbor.

An iBGP connection can occur as long as there is a TCP/IP path between the routers. If multiple paths
exist between the iBGP routers, using a loopback interface as the neighbor address can add stability to the
network. With this command, stability can be achieved by providing the loopback interface address as the
source address of the TCP/IP session.

The no form of this command negates the route updates of the neighbor.

Parameter

Description

Specifies an IP address.

<IP‐ADDRESS>

<PEER‐GROUP‐NAME>

<IPV4>

<IPV6>

Specifies a peer group.

Specifies an interface by IPv4 address.

Specifies an interface by IPv6 address.

loopback <NUMBER>

Specifies a loopback interface number.

Examples

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor 1.1.1.1 update-source loopback 1

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor 1.1.1.1 update-source

switch(config-bgp)# neighbor PG update-source loopback 1

switch(config-bgp)# no neighbor PG update-source

Public

neighbor update-source 789
